resources.string:
These rules apply to the text content of SMS, MMS, or notifications.

<b> - Some typical patterns</b>:
  - Contains \"verification\": .*verification.*
  - Contains any of the words: .*(police|hospital|verification).*

A quick way to copy content from SMS messages or phone numbers, making it easy to grab verification codes or incoming numbers.

When it matches, a button like <b>Copy: 123456</b> will appear in the notification.

A sample rule for extracting verification codes:
  Using capturing group:
   code.*?(\\d+)
  Another approach using lookaround:
   (?<=code.{0,3})\\d+

Choose which of Calls, SMS, MMS, Notification Title, and Notification Body this rule applies to.
Uncheck all of them to disable the rule without deleting it.

When <b>Number</b> is checked, this rule will extract content from the incoming number. For example, use (.*) to copy the phone number.

When <b>Content</b> is checked, this rule will extract content from the SMS message body, such as verification codes.

When Passed is checked, this rule will apply to passed calls and messages.

When Blocked is checked, this rule will apply to blocked calls and messages.

This helps to check if the rules are set as expected. It is just a simulation and will not actually pop up an incoming call, text message, or notification.

<b>Call</b>
Phone Number and Caller Name are tested against Basic Rules, Number Rules, and Content Rules that apply to calls.

<b>SMS</b>
Phone Number and SMS Content are tested against Basic Rules, Number Rules, and Content Rules that apply to SMS/MMS.

<b>Notification</b>
Phone Number is tested against Basic Rules and Number Rules, same as a call/SMS. Title and Body are each tested independently against Number/Text Rules with Title/Body enabled under Apply to Notifications.

The result is shown here, in the history log, and in notifications.
